# Odock.ai — Full Content Index for LLM Crawlers
# https://www.odock.ai

## What Odock is

Odock.ai is an open-source AI governance gateway that sits between applications
and LLM providers or MCP servers. Every request passes through a six-stage
lifecycle: authenticate, authorize, inspect, reserve budget, route, record.

Odock is OpenAI-compatible. Migration requires zero code changes — teams point
existing API calls to the Odock endpoint and issue virtual API keys.

## Who it is for

- Platform engineering teams managing multi-provider LLM infrastructure
- Security teams enforcing prompt injection protection, DLP, and access controls
- FinOps teams enforcing AI spend budgets and quotas per team or project
- Compliance teams needing audit-ready logs for EU AI Act high-risk provisions

## Key capabilities

- Virtual API keys with scoped access per team, user, project, or tenant
- Budget and quota enforcement before upstream calls fire (not post-hoc)
- MCP server governance: tool allowlists, access policies, audit trails
- Prompt injection protection and data leakage controls at the gateway layer
- Multi-provider routing, failover, and load balancing (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)
- EU AI Act compliance support: structured audit evidence for every request
- Plugin architecture for custom workflow steps
- Deployment: cloud-hosted, self-hosted, or hybrid (VPC data plane)

## Documentation

Full documentation: https://docs.odock.ai/docs/getting-started

- Homepage: https://www.odock.ai
- Enterprise: https://www.odock.ai/enterprise/

Demo requests: https://www.odock.ai/contact/
Email: hello@odock.ai

## Blog articles

### What Is an LLM Gateway and Why AI Teams Need One Before Production
URL: https://www.odock.ai/blog/what-is-an-llm-gateway-and-why-ai-teams-need-one/
Summary: Explains what an LLM gateway does, why governance becomes necessary after
the prototype phase, and how Odock centralizes provider access, security, budgets,
and observability behind one endpoint.

### How to Control LLM Costs with Virtual API Keys, Budgets, and Quotas
URL: https://www.odock.ai/blog/how-to-control-llm-costs-with-virtual-api-keys-budgets-and-quotas/
Summary: Explains the operational model for AI cost governance — virtual API keys
for attribution, budget reservations before upstream calls, and real-time quota
enforcement.

### Prompt Injection, Data Leakage, and Why LLM Guardrails Must Live in the Gateway
URL: https://www.odock.ai/blog/prompt-injection-data-leakage-and-llm-security-guardrails/
Summary: Why application-level AI security is insufficient at scale, and how
centralizing prompt injection protection, jailbreak filtering, and DLP controls
at the gateway layer ensures consistent, auditable enforcement.

### LiteLLM, Kong, Cloudflare, Portkey, and Odock: An Honest AI Gateway Comparison
URL: https://www.odock.ai/blog/litellm-kong-cloudflare-portkey-vs-odock-ai-gateway-comparison/
Summary: Compares major AI gateways on philosophy, architecture, and feature set.
Odock's differentiator is modular AI workflow governance vs. routing-first or
API-management-first approaches.

### MCP Server Governance: How to Give AI Agents Tool Access Without Losing Control
URL: https://www.odock.ai/blog/mcp-server-governance-for-ai-agents/
Summary: How to govern MCP tool access for AI agents — allowlists, access
policies, audit trails, and permission scoping at the infrastructure layer.

### How to Design Multi-Provider LLM Routing and Failover Before an Outage
URL: https://www.odock.ai/blog/how-to-design-multi-provider-llm-routing-and-failover/
Summary: A practical guide to multi-provider routing strategies, failover
configuration, and why permissions, budgets, and observability must be in place
before failover is meaningful.

### What to Log, Monitor, and Trace in Production LLM Applications
URL: https://www.odock.ai/blog/what-to-log-monitor-and-trace-in-production-llm-apps/
Summary: A production observability guide covering what to log (quality, latency,
cost, safety, routing), how to trace requests across providers and tools, and
how gateway-level logging differs from application-level logging.
